metadata.title: Homeostasis During Fasting. II. Hormone Substrate Differences Between Men and Women
metadata.journal: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Comparative fasting study with controlled intervention
metadata.demographics: Ten normal men and 9 normal women; Four men matched with 4 women in weight and age; Five women fasted in both preovulatory and postovulatory phases

evidence.quote: Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
Five of the women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle.
The mean lowest glucose concentration in plasma in the male group during the 3 days of starvation was 66.4 ± 2.9 mg/100 ml. The corresponding figure in females was 47.8 ±2.9 mg/100 ml (p < 0.01).
Mean peak glucagon concentrations in women and men were, respectively, 230.0 ± 36.1 mig/ml and 121.8 ± 17.9 nng/ml (p < 0.01).
Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.

methods_participants.label: Glucose assay - coefficient of variation within assay
methods_participants.value: 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding

evidence.quote: The former study was conducted 4 to 5 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbyhodrate
Each 3-day fast was preceded by a 2-day control period.
Samples were obtained at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.

methods_participants.label: Glucose assay - coefficient of variation between assays
methods_participants.value: modifications of Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson method

methods_participants.label: Ethinylestradiol Dosing
methods_participants.value: modifications of Berson and Yalow method

evidence.quote: FFA were measured in duplicate by the method of Trout, Estes and Friedberg using palmitic acid standards and a 4 point standard curve

methods_participants.label: Insulin Assay - Maximum Variation in Basal Range

evidence.quote: Maximum variation in the basal range (0 to 15) was 1.0 u.U/ml.

methods_participants.label: Inclusion Criterion - Hepatic Function
methods_participants.value: No subject was receiving any type of medication

evidence.quote: No subject had CHO intolerance and hepatic screening tests were normal.
Within 10% of ideal body weight according to New York Metropolitan Tables.
For glucagon measurements, samples were run in triplicate with appropriate control tubes for each sample.

narrative_results.statement: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight

evidence.quote: men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes HGH assay replication method (duplicate), while Pass 2 describes duplicate variation measurement (0.5 ng/ml in 0-5 range). Different facts entirely
Pass 1 describes low range variation (0.5 ng/ml in 0-5 range), Pass 2 describes high range variation (1.0 ng/ml in 5-15 range). These are different measurements
Pass 1 describes HGH assay high range variation (1.0 ng/ml in 5-15 range), Pass 2 describes Insulin assay basal range variation (1.0 u.U/ml). Different assays and ranges

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period (Fig. 2, Table 1).

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men, with the difference being significant at the 1% level [lower in women]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men, with the difference significant at the 1% level [sex-based difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men at 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast (p < 0.01) [higher in women]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01) [sex-based difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women during prolonged fasting, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast
The same women fasted at different periods of the menstrual cycle had similar changes of glucose and glucagon
There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting and probably a greater increase of plasma FFA [sex-based difference]

semantic_groups.rationale: Both report 9 women participants; pass_2 adds descriptor 'normal' which is minor additional context
Both specify 72 hours fasting duration; redundant unit notation in pass_2 is formatting artifact
Both specify 0.1 mg twice daily dosing; redundant unit notation in pass_2 is formatting artifact
Pass_1 reports 4 matched pairs (ambiguous); pass_2 clarifies 4 men and 4 women (8 total subjects). Same underlying count but different interpretation of 'pairs'
Both report 5 women in menstrual cycle fasting study; pass_2 adds specific phase details (preovulatory and postovulatory)
Both specify 4 to 5 days after last menstrual bleeding for preovulatory phase; redundant unit notation in pass_2 is formatting artifact
Both specify 15 to 17 days after last menstrual bleeding for postovulatory phase; redundant unit notation in pass_2 is formatting artifact
Both specify minimum 150 g carbohydrate in pre-fasting diet; minor wording variation ('150 g minimum' vs 'minimum of 150 g')
Both describe 1 g/kg body weight protein requirement; minor capitalization and formatting differences only
Both specify minimum 2 weeks pre-fasting diet duration; 'at least 2 weeks' and '2 weeks minimum' are semantically identical
Both describe 2-day control period before fasting; minor wording variation in naming convention
Identical blood sampling frequency specification; only capitalization differs
Identical glucose assay method specification; only capitalization differs
Both specify 1.5% coefficient of variation within assay; pass_2 includes % symbol while pass_1 separates it, but values are identical
Both specify 2.5% coefficient of variation between assays; minor formatting difference with % symbol placement
Identical FFA assay method specification; only capitalization and formatting differ
Identical content with only capitalization differences in headers
Identical content with only capitalization differences in headers
Pass 1 describes HGH assay replication method (duplicate), while Pass 2 describes duplicate variation measurement (0.5 ng/ml in 0-5 range). Different facts entirely
Pass 1 describes low range variation (0.5 ng/ml in 0-5 range), Pass 2 describes high range variation (1.0 ng/ml in 5-15 range). These are different measurements
Pass 1 describes HGH assay high range variation (1.0 ng/ml in 5-15 range), Pass 2 describes Insulin assay basal range variation (1.0 u.U/ml). Different assays and ranges
Same fact about insulin assay replication method (quadruplicate), different phrasing in headers
Pass 1 contains insulin assay maximum variation data, Pass 2 is empty/missing
Same fact about glucagon assay replication method (triplicate), different phrasing in headers
Both state that subjects did not have CHO intolerance; pass_2 is more explicit but conveys the same inclusion criterion
Both indicate normal hepatic function as inclusion criterion; pass_2 provides more detail but same fact
Both state no medication as inclusion criterion; pass_2 is more explicit but conveys identical requirement
Both state same body weight criterion; pass_2 adds '%' notation which is minor formatting difference
Both report 10 men participants; pass_2 adds descriptor 'normal' which is contextual detail not contradictory
Identical statements about sex-based differences in glucose change during fasting
Same numerical data and statistical significance; minor wording variation in descriptor ('lower in women' vs 'sex-based difference')
